edges.description: Loss of catalytically active LKB1 reduces AMPK activation and removes AMPK-mediated restraint on mTORC1 signaling.
STK11 loss in mesenchymal/stromal compartments impairs TGF-beta-mediated epithelial growth restraint.
intermediate_mechanisms: Mesenchymal STK11 loss and impaired SMAD-dependent paracrine signaling

edges.description: Germline STK11 loss creates the inherited background on which somatic second hits and cooperating events drive malignancy.
edges.target: Somatic STK11 second-hit and malignant transformation

edges.description: Reduced LKB1/AMPK signaling removes inhibitory control over mTORC1 in PJS polyp tissue.
mTORC1 activation promotes epithelial growth programs that contribute to hamartomatous polyp burden.
intermediate_mechanisms: Increased mTORC1-dependent translation and HIF-1alpha/GLUT1 metabolic signaling

edges.description: Loss of stromal TGF-beta-mediated epithelial restraint permits local epithelial and stromal overgrowth.
Elevated prostaglandin signaling may support polyp growth and persistence.
STK11 tumor-suppressor loss plus secondary somatic events produces the high GI carcinoma risk observed in PJS.
edges.source: Somatic STK11 second-hit and malignant transformation

edges.description: STK11 tumor-suppressor loss plus secondary somatic events contributes to the extra-GI breast carcinoma risk observed in PJS.
edges.source: Somatic STK11 second-hit and malignant transformation

edges.description: STK11 tumor-suppressor loss plus secondary somatic events contributes to the pancreatic adenocarcinoma risk observed in PJS.
edges.source: Somatic STK11 second-hit and malignant transformation

edges.description: STK11 tumor-suppressor loss plus secondary somatic events contributes to the gynecologic malignancy risk observed in PJS.
edges.source: Somatic STK11 second-hit and malignant transformation

edges.description: Melanocyte hyperpigmentation produces the oral, lip, periorificial, and acral macules used in clinical recognition of PJS.

edges.target: Somatic STK11 second-hit and malignant transformation

nodes.description: Breast carcinoma is part of the extra-GI malignancy spectrum in women with PJS.

evidence.snippet: HP:0003002 | Breast carcinoma | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: High cancer risk in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome: a systematic review and surveillance recommendations.
evidence.snippet: PJS patients are markedly at risk for several malignancies, in particular gastrointestinal cancers and breast cancer

nodes.description: PJS hamartomatous polyps overexpress COX-2/PTGS2 in epithelial and stromal compartments, increasing local prostaglandin biosynthesis. This supports an investigational chemoprevention rationale for COX-2 inhibition.

evidence.reference_title: Overexpression of cyclooxygenase 2 in hamartomatous polyps of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.
evidence.snippet: COX-2 overexpression was noted in hamartomatous polyp tissue from PJS patients compared with normal control and PJS tissue

nodes.description: COX-2 inhibition with celecoxib is investigational for reducing PJS polyp burden, based on COX-2 overexpression in PJS hamartomas and preclinical rationale; it is not established standard care.

evidence.snippet: Cyclooxygenase (COX) is overexpressed in hamartomatous polyp tissue from PJS individuals, which may provide an avenue for possible effective chemoprevention of polyp formation and growth in PJS

nodes.description: Females with PJS are at risk for adenoma malignum of the cervix, a rare aggressive cervical cancer.

evidence.snippet: HP:0030079 | Cervix cancer | Occasional (29-5%)
Females are at risk for sex cord tumors with annular tubules (SCTAT), a benign neoplasm of the ovaries, and adenoma malignum of the cervix, a rare aggressive cancer.

nodes.description: PJS carries high risk of GI malignancies, including colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, small intestinal, and other epithelial cancers.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002672 | Gastrointestinal carcinoma | Very frequent (99-80%)
Individuals with PJS are at increased risk for a wide variety of epithelial malignancies (colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, breast, and ovarian cancers)

nodes.description: Characteristic hamartomatous polyps throughout the GI tract, especially the small intestine, define the syndrome and cause bleeding, obstruction, and intussusception.

evidence.snippet: Abnormality of the gastrointestinal tract | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome in Children and Adults.
evidence.snippet: presence of hamartomatous polyposis throughout the gastrointestinal tract, except for the esophagus, along with characteristic mucocutaneous pigmentation.

nodes.description: LKB1 normally activates AMPK-family kinases. In PJS polyp tissue, pathogenic STK11 variants reduce phosphorylated AMPK, weakening energy- and polarity-linked growth control and contributing to downstream mTORC1 hyperactivation.

evidence.reference_title: Two missense STK11 gene variations impaired LKB1/adenosine monophosphate-activated protein kinase signaling in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.
evidence.snippet: phosphorylated-AMPK (Thr172) expression was significantly lower in gastric, colonic, and uterine polyps from PJS patients with missense variations than in non-PJS patients

nodes.description: PJS causes characteristic dark blue to brown macules around the mouth, eyes, nostrils, perianal area, buccal mucosa, lips, and extremities. The precise melanocyte-intrinsic mechanism remains incompletely defined, but pigmentation commonly appears in childhood before GI complications.

evidence.snippet: Mucocutaneous hyperpigmentation presents in childhood as dark blue to dark brown macules around the mouth, eyes, and nostrils, in the perianal area, and on the buccal mucosa

nodes.description: Dark macules on the lips, oral mucosa, periorificial skin, and acral sites are a hallmark and often present early in childhood.

evidence.snippet: HP:0100669 | Abnormal pigmentation of the oral mucosa | Very frequent (99-80%)
Mucocutaneous hyperpigmentation presents in childhood as dark blue to dark brown macules around the mouth, eyes, and nostrils, in the perianal area, and on the buccal mucosa

nodes.description: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma is one of the GI and extra-intestinal cancers included in PJS surveillance recommendations.

evidence.snippet: HP:0006725 | Pancreatic adenocarcinoma | Occasional (29-5%)
Individuals with PJS are at increased risk for a wide variety of epithelial malignancies (colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, breast, and ovarian cancers)

nodes.description: Germline heterozygous pathogenic variants in STK11, encoding the LKB1 serine/threonine kinase, initiate PJS. Loss of tumor-suppressor kinase function perturbs epithelial polarity, growth-control, and stromal paracrine signaling, creating the inherited background for hamartomatous polyposis, mucocutaneous pigmentation, and cancer susceptibility.

cell_types: stromal cell of small intestinal lamina propria

evidence.snippet: STK11 | serine/threonine kinase 11 | hgnc:11389 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome in Children and Adults.
evidence.snippet: It is caused by germline pathogenic variants of the STK11 gene, which exhibit an autosomal dominant mode of inheritance
STK11 | serine/threonine kinase 11 | hgnc:11389 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome in Children and Adults.
evidence.snippet: It is caused by germline pathogenic variants of the STK11 gene, which exhibit an autosomal dominant mode of inheritance

evidence.reference_title: mTOR and HIF-1alpha-mediated tumor metabolism in an LKB1 mouse model of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.
evidence.snippet: PJS patients develop gastrointestinal hamartomas with 100% penetrance often in the second decade of life
STK11 | HGNC:11389 | Peutz-Jeghers syndrome | MONDO:0008280 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: mTOR inhibition is investigational in PJS. Mouse-model data show rapamycin suppression of preexisting GI polyps, supporting mechanism-directed interest in the LKB1/AMPK/mTORC1 axis.

evidence.reference_title: mTOR and HIF-1alpha-mediated tumor metabolism in an LKB1 mouse model of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.
evidence.snippet: rapamycin as a single agent results in a dramatic suppression of preexisting GI polyps in LKB1+/- mice

nodes.description: Germline STK11 pathogenic variants confer inherited susceptibility, while somatic loss of the remaining STK11 allele and cooperating oncogenic events drive the high lifetime risk of GI and extra-GI epithelial malignancies.
nodes.id: Somatic STK11 second-hit and malignant transformation

evidence.reference_title: High cancer risk in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome: a systematic review and surveillance recommendations.
evidence.snippet: PJS patients are markedly at risk for several malignancies, in particular gastrointestinal cancers and breast cancer

nodes.description: Mesenchymal/stromal LKB1 loss can drive PJS-like GI polyposis by reducing stromal TGF-beta signaling to the epithelium. This paracrine defect removes a local growth-suppressive brake and helps explain the arborizing stromal architecture of PJS hamartomas.

cell_types: stromal cell of small intestinal lamina propria

evidence.reference_title: LKB1 signaling in mesenchymal cells required for suppression of gastrointestinal polyposis.
evidence.snippet: either monoallelic or biallelic loss of murine Stk11 limited to Tagln-expressing mesenchymal cells results in premature postnatal death as a result of gastrointestinal polyps indistinguishable from those in PJS

evidence.reference_title: LKB1 signaling in mesenchymal cells required for suppression of gastrointestinal polyposis.
evidence.snippet: We also noted TGFbeta signaling defects in polyps of individuals with PJS

nodes.description: LKB1-deficient PJS tissues show increased mTORC1 signaling with elevated HIF-1alpha and GLUT1, supporting proliferative polyp growth and providing a mechanism-directed rationale for investigational mTOR inhibition.

evidence.reference_title: mTOR and HIF-1alpha-mediated tumor metabolism in an LKB1 mouse model of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.
evidence.snippet: polyps from human Peutz-Jeghers patients similarly exhibit up-regulated mTORC1 signaling, HIF-1alpha, and GLUT1 levels.
